· Used phone /bus schedule (Goog Maps)
+ reuseable it's canvas bag

Isomes walks if weather nice has time
+ back home
· SNAP : $291/mon
· Shops at Kwik Trip first to see if any good deals
sometimes gets stuff only there if TJ's closed
· Usually buys beef at Kwik Trip because cheaper
80/20 $4.79 roll for pasta
· Buys rest at Trader Joe's
closer + full service
· Sometimes stops at Stop & Shop (open late) but doesn't accept SNAP
only buys snack there
· Prefers to carry Kwik Trip items to Trader Joe's instead of dropping off at home
prevents overshopping at TJ's + speeds trip
· Brings reusable bag
· Whole trip ~ under an hour (approx)
Interpretations
· Time/effort is key driver (distance + schedule)
· SNAP acceptance shapes store choice
· Uses “physical burden” as behavioral constraint to reduce overbuying
· List reduces cognitive load in overstimulating store environment
Activity / Context / Tools / Artifacts
· Phone used as companion tool
Google Maps for bus schedule (especially return trip)
· Environment: Regent Apartments; shared bedroom + shared fridge with roommate(s)
· Storage constraints limit bulk/long-term meal prep
· Busy schedule (quant major) makes weekly Sunday trip ideal
Needs / Values
· Optimize for: low cost + fresh produce + enough food for 3 meals/day + snacks
· Weekly budget goal: $40–$50 groceries
· Uses remaining SNAP funds for snacks + 1–2 freezer meals for “IDGAF days”
· Tries to reduce meat consumption sometimes (beans/lentils cheaper protein)
Beliefs / Expectations
· Assumes “nothing really gets better” by shopping around (time > small savings)
· Trader Joe's seen as best balance of convenience + price for their situation
· Doesn’t vary routine unless special occasion cooking (Midway Asian Foods, Mercado Marimar, etc.)
Breakdowns / Successes
Successes:
· Highly systematized weekly routine
· Uses list + store sequence to manage temptation/overwhelm
· Uses transit smartly (walk there, bus back)
{'n': 1, 'text': 'Strong “system” reduces decision fatigue: pantry survey → list → KT price-check → TJ bulk buy → bus home.'}
{'n': 2, 'text': 'Constraints (SNAP acceptance, distance, transit, shared fridge) *shape* shopping behavior more than “best price.”'}
{'n': 3, 'text': 'Clever self-control strategy: carry early purchases to reduce overshopping + speed time in-store.'}
Breakdowns:
· Overstimulation/long lines at TJ’s on Sunday brunch hours
· Carrying groceries is “hell” (drives bus use)
· Limited fridge space (shared)
Notable Quotes (approx / paraphrased if OCR missed)
· “Carrying groceries back is kind of hell.”
· “Trader Joe’s is usually as cheap as it gets for my living situation.”
· “I get distracted and buy too much otherwise.”
Photos Taken (from Ale)
· Grocery list note screenshot
· Street/walking context photos
· Bus stop photo
· Kwik Trip meat cooler photo
· TJ produce aisle photo
· TJ rice aisle photo
· TJ bread aisle photo
Top Insights
Strong “system” reduces decision fatigue: pantry survey → list → KT price-check → TJ bulk buy → bus home.
Constraints (SNAP acceptance, distance, transit, shared fridge) *shape* shopping behavior more than “best price.”
Clever self-control strategy: carry early purchases to reduce overshopping + speed time in-store.
Biggest Surprise
· Extremely consistent routine; minimal store switching or delivery; little experimentation once system works.
Questions / Follow-up
· How often does overshopping happen without the “carry-first” strategy?
· How does the shared fridge affect conflicts, waste, and planning?
· What would improve the experience most: list tool, price comparison, SNAP filter, transit planning, or storage visibility?

Contextual Inquiry Field Notes
Time: 1:26 PM
Location: Student Activities Center, 333 University Ave
Participant ID: P1 — Iris
Facilitator: Helena Huang
Note Taker: Yanbin Chen
Activity Being Observed:
The process of how participants (college students) organize and plan grocery shopping in their daily life.
Participant is an international student.
Mentions weather conditions sometimes affect whether she goes out to shop (very cold days may cancel plans).
Knows about the food pantry available on the UW-Madison campus but has never used it.
Previously paid attention to food prices and sometimes compared prices with stores in her hometown, but does not actively do this anymore.
Usually goes directly to grocery stores that are close to her daily locations.
Specifically mentions Fresh Market as a convenient store near her apartment.
Goes grocery shopping almost every day to buy fresh vegetables rather than doing one large weekly trip.
Sometimes visits Target to purchase specific items such as blueberries.
Grocery shopping decisions depend on her daily schedule and convenience (for example stopping by stores on the way to class).
Emphasizes quality of food first, convenience second.
If prices are the same, she prefers higher quality food.
If quality is similar across options, she will choose the cheaper price.
Participant likely receives financial support from family.
Convenience is an important factor in grocery decisions.
Price awareness may decrease over time once students become familiar with local prices.
Personal preference strongly influences store choice.
Participant prioritizes fresh food on a daily basis rather than bulk purchases.
Grocery shopping behavior is integrated into daily routines instead of planned in advance.
Observation Notes
Observations (What you see / hear)
Participant is an international student.
Mentions weather conditions sometimes affect whether she goes out to shop (very cold days may cancel plans).
Knows about the food pantry available on the UW-Madison campus but has never used it.
Previously paid attention to food prices and sometimes compared prices with stores in her hometown, but does not actively do this anymore.
Usually goes directly to grocery stores that are close to her daily locations.
Specifically mentions Fresh Market as a convenient store near her apartment.
Goes grocery shopping almost every day to buy fresh vegetables rather than doing one large weekly trip.
Sometimes visits Target to purchase specific items such as blueberries.
Grocery shopping decisions depend on her daily schedule and convenience (for example stopping by stores on the way to class).
Emphasizes quality of food first, convenience second.
If prices are the same, she prefers higher quality food.
If quality is similar across options, she will choose the cheaper price.
Interpretations (What it might mean)
Participant likely receives financial support from family.
Convenience is an important factor in grocery decisions.
Price awareness may decrease over time once students become familiar with local prices.
Personal preference strongly influences store choice.
Participant prioritizes fresh food on a daily basis rather than bulk purchases.
Grocery shopping behavior is integrated into daily routines instead of planned in advance.

The main discussion takes place in the UW-Madison Student Activities Center because the participant chose it as a comfortable interview location.
The environment is relatively quiet, with only a few people talking in the background.
The primary tool used during the planning process is the participant's smartphone.
The participant demonstrates how she browses grocery delivery applications on her phone.
The participant also physically visits Fresh Market during the session to illustrate how she normally shops in person.
The observation includes both digital interaction (mobile app) and physical store interaction.
Food quality is the most important factor.
Freshness of food is also highly valued.
The participant prefers groceries that are recently stocked and appear fresh.
She integrates grocery trips into daily routines for convenience.
Frequent small trips allow her to maintain food freshness rather than storing food long-term.
We assume the participant tries to save time by combining grocery shopping with other daily activities.
Shopping during normal routines (walking to class, returning home) reduces the need for separate grocery trips.
Areas to Document
ACTIVITY — What is the participant doing? (step-by-step actions)
The participant begins by describing her usual grocery shopping routine.
She explains that she rarely plans far ahead and instead shops frequently during the week. (approx. 3 minutes)
She opens the grocery delivery application on her phone. (about 10 seconds)
She navigates through the application and shows items she normally purchases online.
She adds several of these common items into the shopping cart while demonstrating the interface.
This process lasts roughly 2 minutes.
The participant then walks with the researchers to Fresh Market.
She demonstrates what her normal shopping trip there looks like and points out typical items she buys.
This observational portion lasts approximately 15 minutes.
CONTEXT — Where is this happening? What tools, artifacts, people, environmental factors are present?
The main discussion takes place in the UW-Madison Student Activities Center because the participant chose it as a comfortable interview location.
The environment is relatively quiet, with only a few people talking in the background.
The primary tool used during the planning process is the participant's smartphone.
The participant demonstrates how she browses grocery delivery applications on her phone.
The participant also physically visits Fresh Market during the session to illustrate how she normally shops in person.
The observation includes both digital interaction (mobile app) and physical store interaction.
NEEDS / VALUES — What matters most to the participant?
Food quality is the most important factor.
Freshness of food is also highly valued.
The participant prefers groceries that are recently stocked and appear fresh.
She integrates grocery trips into daily routines for convenience.
Frequent small trips allow her to maintain food freshness rather than storing food long-term.
ASSUMPTIONS — What beliefs guide their behavior?
We assume the participant tries to save time by combining grocery shopping with other daily activities.
Shopping during normal routines (walking to class, returning home) reduces the need for separate grocery trips.
The participant occasionally pauses in the store while thinking about which items she needs that day.
This happens multiple times during the Fresh Market visit.
The lack of a predefined list means she must remember items while browsing the store.
Decision making sometimes occurs spontaneously rather than being planned.
The participant smoothly navigates the grocery delivery application.
She quickly finds items she regularly purchases and adds them to the cart.
This suggests she is very familiar with the interface and the products she normally buys.
Breakdowns & Successes
BREAKDOWNS (frustrations, confusion)
The participant occasionally pauses in the store while thinking about which items she needs that day.
This happens multiple times during the Fresh Market visit.
The lack of a predefined list means she must remember items while browsing the store.
Decision making sometimes occurs spontaneously rather than being planned.
SUCCESSES (what worked well)
The participant smoothly navigates the grocery delivery application.
She quickly finds items she regularly purchases and adds them to the cart.
This suggests she is very familiar with the interface and the products she normally buys.
I prefer directly going to stores that are close to my daily activity locations, like Fresh Market.
I've gone to many different stores just to find the most delicious blueberries because I really like blueberries and I want them to be fresh and high quality.
Notable Quotes
"I prefer directly going to stores that are close to my daily activity locations, like Fresh Market."
"I've gone to many different stores just to find the most delicious blueberries because I really like blueberries and I want them to be fresh and high quality."
Photos Taken
Listed in the accompanying photo documentation.
{'n': 1, 'text': "Food quality (freshness and taste) is the most important factor influencing the participant's grocery decisions."}
{'n': 2, 'text': 'Price still matters, but it is less critical because the participant has financial support from her family as an international student.'}
{'n': 3, 'text': 'The planning phase of grocery shopping is relatively short.'}
Post-Session Reflection
Top 3 Insights from this Session
Food quality (freshness and taste) is the most important factor influencing the participant's grocery decisions.
Price still matters, but it is less critical because the participant has financial support from her family as an international student.
The planning phase of grocery shopping is relatively short.
Many decisions occur during the shopping trip itself rather than beforehand.
Biggest Surprise
The participant purchases groceries in between daily activities not primarily to save time but to ensure that the food she buys is fresh.

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: Q: Would you prefer shopping at Fresh Market or at a store closer to your home?

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: A: The participant says she would still choose Fresh Market because it is more convenient to stop there between daily activities and the route fits naturally into her schedule.

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: Q: Is there a time when you seriously consider food prices when buying groceries?

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: A: When she first arrived in the United States she compared prices frequently with those in her home country.

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: Now that she is familiar with local prices she does not focus as heavily on price unless it is unusually high.

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: How this Connects to Our Focus Objectives

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: College students may care strongly about food quality and freshness.

section_heading: Questions for Follow-up / Things to Explore
text: A grocery-planning application could potentially include information about store restocking times so users know when the freshest products are available.
International students may prioritize food quality more than price if they receive financial support from family.
Travel time to grocery stores is an important factor influencing store choice and should be considered in design solutions.

Additional Session Field Notes
Participant: Franky (Graduate student, lives off campus, no meal plan)
Duration: 38 minutes
Note Taker: Peixin Yang
0–5 minutes
Participant opens phone.
States: "I usually just buy the same stuff every week."
Begins listing common grocery items:
eggs
milk
rice
chicken
yogurt
Participant appears relaxed while describing routine.
5–12 minutes
Participant opens Target website on laptop.
Searches for "chicken breast".
There is a long pause (~20 seconds) while comparing two options.
Participant says:
"This one is cheaper... I'll probably get that."
Manually compares price per pound.
Observation:
Participant is very price sensitive and currently has no tool assisting price comparison.
12–20 minutes
Participant does not check weekly ads before selecting items.
Notices the "sale" label only after clicking the item page.
Participant says:
"I usually forget to check sales honestly."
Participant appears slightly annoyed.
20–30 minutes
Participant adds items to cart.
Checks subtotal which is slightly above $60.
Participant sighs and says:
"I try to keep it under $60."
Deletes chips and ice cream from cart.
Participant comments:
"Snacks are the first to go."
Observation:
Budget pressure influences decisions near checkout.
30–38 minutes
Participant performs mental math instead of using a calculator.
States:
"I just kinda guess most of the time."
There is no tracking of previous weeks' grocery spending.
Participant appears uncertain about the final total price.
Design Opportunities
Real-time grocery budget tracker.
Automatic highlighting of sale items.
Weekly spending summary.
Suggestions for cheaper substitute products.
